content:
# John Chapter 3 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 3 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 3:1 — "a man of the Pharisees named Nicodemus" vs. "a Pharisee and a member of the Jewish ruling council"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there was a man of the Pharisaioi, Nikodemos [was] the name of him, a ruler of the Ioudaioi" | Literal; transliterated names; Greek structure |
| LOGOS | "there was a man of the Pharisees named Nicodemus, a ruler of the Jews" | Formal; clear English |
| PNEUMA | "There was a man named Nicodemus, a Pharisee and a member of the Jewish ruling council." | Dynamic; interprets "ruler of the Jews" as "member of the Jewish ruling council" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "member of the Jewish ruling council" is interpretive (correctly identifying the Sanhedrin, but adding specificity not in the text).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:2 — "a teacher come from God" vs. "God has sent you to teach us"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "from God you have come [as] a teacher, for no one is able to do these signs that you do unless the God is with him" | Literal; preserves Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "you are a teacher come from God, for no one can do these signs that you do unless God is with him" | Formal; standard English |
| PNEUMA | "God has sent you to teach us. Your miraculous signs prove that God is with you." | Dynamic; splits into two sentences; adds "miraculous" and "prove" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "miraculous signs" adds an adjective not in the Greek (John carefully uses "signs," not "miraculous signs"). "Prove" is also stronger than the Greek warrant.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:3 — "Truly, truly" vs. "I tell you the solemn truth" for Greek ἀμὴν ἀμήν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Truly, truly I say to you" | Literal double-amen |
| LOGOS | "Truly, truly, I say to you" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "I tell you the solemn truth" | Dynamic equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed EMET/LOGOS — consistent rendering of the Johannine double-amen formula throughout. (Same pattern as 1:51.)

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Recurring pattern.

---

## 3:4 — "How can a man be born when he is old?" — rendering style

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How is a man able to be born, being old? Is he able to enter into the womb of the mother of him a second [time] and be born?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "How can a man be born when he is old? Can he enter a second time into his mother's womb and be born?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "'How can an old man be born again?' Nicodemus asked. 'Surely he can't go back into his mother's womb and be born a second time!'" | Dynamic; adds "Nicodemus asked"; rhetorical exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds narrative attribution ("Nicodemus asked") mid-verse and converts the question to an exclamation ("Surely he can't!"), adding rhetorical force not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:6 — "That which is born of the flesh is flesh" vs. "Flesh gives birth to flesh"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The [thing] having been born of the flesh is flesh, and the [thing] having been born of the Spirit is spirit." | Literal; participle structure |
| LOGOS | "That which is born of the flesh is flesh, and that which is born of the Spirit is spirit." | Formal; elegant parallel |
| PNEUMA | "Flesh gives birth to flesh, but the Spirit gives birth to spirit." | Dynamic; restructures as active voice; adds "but" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's active-voice restructuring is clear but changes the grammatical logic: the Greek emphasizes what the offspring IS (a state of being), not what the parent DOES (an act of generation). The adversative "but" is not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:8 — "where it wishes" vs. "wherever it pleases" for Greek ὅπου θέλει

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The wind blows where it wills, and the sound of it you hear" | Literal; "wills"; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "The wind blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound" | Formal; English word order |
| PNEUMA | "The wind blows wherever it pleases. You hear its sound, but you can't tell where it comes from or where it's going." | Dynamic; "wherever it pleases"; "can't tell" instead of "do not know" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "can't tell" narrows "do not know" (οὐκ οἶδας) and the sentence splitting diminishes the flowing quality of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:9-10 — "How can these things be?" vs. "How is this possible?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "'How are these things able to be?'...'You are the teacher of the Israel and you do not know these things?'" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "'How can these things be?'...'Are you the teacher of Israel and yet you do not understand these things?'" | Formal; "understand" |
| PNEUMA | "'How is this possible?'...'You are Israel's teacher, and you don't understand these things?'" | Dynamic; "How is this possible?"; possessive "Israel's" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. Minor differences; LOGOS balances faithfulness with clarity. Note: LOGOS uses "understand" for γινώσκεις where EMET uses "know" — both are valid for this verb.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 3:11 — "bear witness to what we have seen" vs. "testify about what we have seen"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "what we know we speak, and what we have seen we testify, and the testimony of us you do not receive" | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "we speak of what we know, and bear witness to what we have seen, but you do not receive our testimony" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "we speak about what we know and testify about what we have seen, and yet you people refuse to accept our testimony" | Dynamic; "you people" adds pejorative; "refuse to accept" stronger than "do not receive" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "you people" adds a collective and slightly antagonistic tone. "Refuse to accept" implies active resistance beyond what "do not receive" (οὐ λαμβάνετε) strictly conveys.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:13 — "No one has ascended into heaven" — omission of "who is in heaven"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "no one has ascended into the heaven except the [one] having descended from the heaven — the Son of the Man" | Follows Sinaiticus; omits later addition |
| LOGOS | "No one has ascended into heaven except he who descended from heaven, the Son of Man." | Same; follows Sinaiticus |
| PNEUMA | "No one has ever gone up to heaven except the one who came down from heaven — the Son of Man." | Dynamic; "gone up" / "came down" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** All three correctly follow the shorter Sinaiticus reading (omitting "who is in heaven"). PNEUMA's "gone up" / "came down" simplifies "ascended" / "descended" — acceptable but loses the theological register.

**Status:** Consensus on text. PNEUMA's vocabulary is more casual.

---

## 3:14 — "Moses lifted up the serpent" vs. "Moses lifted up the bronze snake on a pole"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "just as Mouses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "just as Moses lifted up the bronze snake on a pole in the wilderness" | Dynamic; adds "bronze" and "on a pole" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "bronze" and "on a pole" — details from Numbers 21:9 that are accurate but not present in John's text. The translator should not harmonize across books.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:16 — "For God so loved the world" vs. "For this is how God loved the world"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For so the God loved the world that the Son, the only-begotten, he gave" | Literal; Greek word order; "only-begotten" |
| LOGOS | "For God so loved the world, that he gave his only Son" | Formal; traditional rendering |
| PNEUMA | "For this is how God loved the world: he gave his one and only Son" | Dynamic; renders οὕτως as "this is how"; "one and only" for μονογενής |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "For God so loved the world" is arguably the most recognized sentence in English-language Christianity. PNEUMA's "this is how God loved" more accurately captures the adverbial force of οὕτως (manner, not just degree), but the traditional rendering is too embedded in the language to displace. EMET's word order and "only-begotten" preserve the Greek structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA's insight on οὕτως noted in council notes.

---

## 3:17 — "condemn" vs. "judge" for Greek κρίνῃ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that he might judge the world" | Literal; "judge" for κρίνω |
| LOGOS | "to condemn the world" | Formal; "condemn" |
| PNEUMA | "to condemn the world" | Dynamic; same as LOGOS |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS/PNEUMA — "condemn." While κρίνω can mean simply "judge," the context (contrasted with "save") makes "condemn" the appropriate English rendering.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/PNEUMA.

---

## 3:18 — "only Son of God" vs. "only-begotten Son" vs. "God's one and only Son" for μονογενοῦς υἱοῦ τοῦ θεοῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the only-begotten Son of the God" | Literal; "only-begotten" |
| LOGOS | "the only Son of God" | Formal; "only" |
| PNEUMA | "God's one and only Son" | Dynamic; "one and only" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. EMET's "only-begotten" preserves the etymological nuance of μονογενής but is debated. PNEUMA's "one and only" is essentially equivalent to LOGOS's "only" but wordier.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:19 — "the judgment" vs. "the basis for judgment" for Greek ἡ κρίσις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "this is the judgment: that the light has come..." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "this is the judgment: the light has come..." | Formal; same |
| PNEUMA | "this is the basis for judgment: the light has come..." | Dynamic; adds "basis for" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "basis for" is interpretive — the Greek simply says "this is the judgment" (αὕτη ἐστὶν ἡ κρίσις).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:20 — "everyone who does wicked things" vs. "everyone who does evil" vs. "everyone practicing worthless things"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "everyone practicing worthless [things]" | Literal; φαῦλα as "worthless" |
| LOGOS | "everyone who does wicked things" | Formal; "wicked" |
| PNEUMA | "Everyone who does evil" | Dynamic; "evil" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. φαῦλος means "base, worthless, evil" — all three renderings are within range. "Wicked things" is clear without being as casual as "evil."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 3:21 — "does what is true" vs. "doing the truth" vs. "live by the truth" for ποιῶν τὴν ἀλήθειαν

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] doing the truth comes to the light" | Literal; "doing the truth" |
| LOGOS | "whoever does what is true comes to the light" | Formal; "does what is true" |
| PNEUMA | "those who live by the truth come to the light" | Dynamic; "live by the truth" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. EMET's "doing the truth" is the most literal and preserves a distinctively Johannine/Qumran phrase (ποιεῖν τὴν ἀλήθειαν). PNEUMA's "live by the truth" shifts from action to lifestyle.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. EMET's literalism noted as preserving an important idiom.

---

## 3:25 — "a discussion" vs. "a debate" vs. "a dispute" for Greek ζήτησις

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "there arose a dispute from the disciples of Ioannes with a Ioudaios" | "Dispute" |
| LOGOS | "a discussion arose between some of John's disciples and a Jew" | "Discussion" |
| PNEUMA | "A debate broke out between John's disciples and a certain Jew" | "Debate broke out" — more dramatic |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — "discussion." ζήτησις means inquiry/discussion/debate. "Discussion" is neutral; "dispute" and "debate broke out" add intensity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:27 — "A person cannot receive" vs. "A man is not able to receive" vs. "No one can receive"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "A man is not able to receive anything unless it has been given to him from the heaven." | Literal; "man"; "the heaven" |
| LOGOS | "A person cannot receive even one thing unless it is given him from heaven." | Formal; gender-neutral "person" |
| PNEUMA | "No one can receive anything unless God gives it from heaven." | Dynamic; adds "God" as explicit subject |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's addition of "God" as explicit subject is interpretive — the Greek uses a passive construction (divine passive), leaving God implicit.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:29 — "rejoices greatly" vs. "rejoices with joy" vs. "stands by and listens with great joy"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the friend of the bridegroom, the [one] standing and hearing him, rejoices with joy because of the voice of the bridegroom. This therefore, the joy of me, has been fulfilled." | Literal; "rejoices with joy" (cognate dative); "has been fulfilled" |
| LOGOS | "The friend of the bridegroom, who stands and hears him, rejoices greatly at the bridegroom's voice. Therefore this joy of mine is now complete." | Formal; "complete" |
| PNEUMA | "The best man stands by and listens with great joy to the bridegroom's voice. That is my joy, and it is now complete." | Dynamic; "best man" for "friend of the bridegroom" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA's "best man" modernizes a culturally specific role; EMET's "fulfilled" (πεπλήρωται) is literally "has been filled/completed." LOGOS's "complete" captures the meaning well.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:30 — "He must increase, but I must decrease" vs. "He must become greater and greater, and I must become less and less"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "That [one] must increase, but I must decrease." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "He must increase, but I must decrease." | Formal; identical in substance |
| PNEUMA | "He must become greater and greater, and I must become less and less." | Dynamic; doubles the comparative for emphasis |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS — the simple "increase/decrease" is powerful in its brevity. PNEUMA's doubling ("greater and greater / less and less") adds progressive emphasis not in the Greek, which uses simple infinitives (αὐξάνειν / ἐλαττοῦσθαι).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 3:34 — "utters the words of God" vs. "speaks God's words" for Greek λαλεῖ τὰ ῥήματα τοῦ θεοῦ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] whom the God sent speaks the words of the God" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "he whom God has sent utters the words of God" | Formal; "utters" |
| PNEUMA | "the one God has sent speaks God's words" | Dynamic; possessive "God's words" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Utters" is slightly more formal than "speaks" but both are valid for λαλέω. Minor difference.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minimal divergence.

---

## 3:36 — "does not obey the Son" vs. "disobeying the Son" vs. "refuses to obey the Son" for Greek ἀπειθῶν τῷ υἱῷ

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] disobeying the Son will not see life" | Literal; participle |
| LOGOS | "whoever does not obey the Son shall not see life" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Anyone who refuses to obey the Son will never experience eternal life" | Dynamic; "refuses to" adds willfulness; "experience" instead of "see" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. ἀπειθέω means to disobey/be disobedient — PNEUMA's "refuses to obey" adds intentional willfulness. "Experience" for "see" (ὄψεται) loses the Johannine visual metaphor.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words/details) | 9 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 7 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure / transliteration | 8 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Double-amen formula rendering | 3 | Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS |
| Word choice divergence (all three) | 6 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Textual variant (Sinaiticus shorter reading) | 1 | Consensus |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 3 contains some of the most theologically loaded language in the NT, and the divergences reflect each translator's philosophy applied to high-stakes material. The most significant divergence is on 3:16: PNEUMA's "this is how God loved" more faithfully captures the Greek adverb οὕτως but was overruled in favor of the traditional "God so loved" — one of the rare cases where cultural embeddedness outweighs strict accuracy. EMET's preservation of "only-begotten" (μονογενής) raises the perennial debate about this word's etymology versus its functional meaning in context. PNEUMA's tendency to add narrative attributions, interpretive glosses ("bronze snake on a pole"), and intensifying words ("refuses to," "you people") continues from earlier chapters.

All three translators agree on the critical rendering of ἄνωθεν as "from above" rather than "born again," which represents a significant consensus against the dominant English tradition.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
